abstract:
This article examines whether political parties respond to the issue priorities of voters when drafting their election manifestos. Moving beyond traditional studies of positional congruence, the authors investigate the conditions under which parties adjust their thematic focus to match the concerns of the electorate. Analyzing a comprehensive dataset of 130 parties across eighteen European democracies between 1972 and 2011, the study combines Comparative Manifestos Project data with "most important problem" voter surveys. 

The findings reveal that while parties generally respond to shifts in voter attention with a time lag, this responsiveness is significantly moderated by party characteristics. Larger "catch-all" parties demonstrate greater responsiveness to the general public’s priorities compared to smaller parties. Conversely, governing parties are found to be less responsive than those in opposition, likely due to the policy constraints and credibility stakes associated with holding office. Finally, the research clarifies the behavior of niche parties; while they are not more responsive to the public at large, they show a heightened sensitivity to the specific priorities of their own supporters within their "owned" issue domains, such as environmental protection for Green parties. These results offer critical insights into the mechanisms of democratic linkage and the diverse strategies parties employ to maintain electoral relevance.
